name: CHEMISTRY (Theory)
question_paper_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/5/2 (set-2)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 11 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper comprises of three sections- Section A, B and C.
  3. Section A - Q. No. 1 to 3 are very short-answer type questions carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section B - Q. No. 4 to 11 are short-answer type questions carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section C - Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is not allowed.

SECTION A
1.

Arrange the following in the increasing order of their property indicated:

  1. Ethanal, Propanone, Propanal, Butanone (reactivity towards nucleophilic addition)
  2. 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, benzoic acid, 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid, 4-Methoxy benzoic acid (Acid strength)
 2
2.

Explain the following reactions:

  1. Clemmensen reduction
  2. Stephen reaction
 2
3.

Answer the following questions (Do any two):

  1. Identify the order of reaction from the following unit for its rate constant: Lmol-1s-1
  2. The conversion of molecules A to B follow second order kinetics. concentration of A is increased to three times, how will it affect the rate of formation of B?
  3. Write the expression of integrated rate equation for zero order reaction.
 2

SECTION B
4.(a)
  1. Define coagulation.
  2. State Hardy-Schulze rule.
  3. What is Electrophoresis?
 3

OR
(b)

Write three differences between Physisorption and Chemisorption.

 3
5.(a)
  1. Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: [Pt(NH3)6]Cl4
  2. On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration of d4 ion, if {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} < P.
  3. What are Heteroleptic complexes?
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Using IUPAC norms write the formulas for the following:
    1. Pentaamminenitrito-N-Cobalt (III)
    2. Tetrahydroxidozincate (II)
  2. What is crystal field splitting energy?
 3
6.
  1. Write any two consequences of Lanthanoid Contraction.
  2. Name the element of 3d series which exhibits the largest number of oxidation states. Give reason.
 3
7.

Observe the graph shown in figure and answer the following questions:

  1. What is the order of the reaction?
  2. What is the slope of the curve?
  3. Write the relationship between k and t1/2 (half life period)
 3
8.

Give reasons for the following statements:

  1. Scandium (Z = 21) is a transition element but Zn (Z = 30) is not.
  2. [Ti(H2O)]3+ is coloured while [Sc(H2 O)6]3+ is colourless.
  3. Physical and chemical properties of the 4d and 5d series of the transition elements are quite similar than expected.
 3
9.(a)

Complete the following:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{2\cdot{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{1. \mathrm{AlH}(\mathrm i-\mathrm{Bu})_2}{/tex} {tex}\text{ 'A' }\xrightarrow[\mathrm H^+]{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm N-\mathrm{OH}}\text{ 'B' }\\{/tex}
  2. Write IUPAC name of the following compound:
  3. Write chemical test to distinguish between the following compounds: Phenol and Benzoic acid
 3

OR
(b)

Convert the following:

  1. Benzoic acid to Benzaldehyde
  2. Propan-1-ol to 2-Bromopropanoic acid
  3. Acetaldehyde to But-2-enal
 3
10.

A primary amine A C2H7N reacts with alkyl halide (C2H5I) to give secondary amine B. B reacts with C6H5SO2Cl to give a solid C which is insoluble in alkali. Identify A, B, C and write all the chemical reactions involved.

 3
11.(a)

Account for the following:

  1. pkb of aniline is more than that of methylamine.
  2. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  3. Primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines.
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their basic strength in aqueous solution:
    CH3 NH2, (CH3)3 N, (CH3)2 NH
  2. What is Hinsberg's reagent?
  3. What is the role of pyridine in the acylation reaction of amines?
 3

SECTION C
12.

Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow:

Oxidation-reduction reactions are commonly known as redox reactions. They involve transfer of electrons from one species to another. In a spontaneous reaction, energy is released which can be used to do useful work. The reaction is split into two half-reactions. Two different containers are used and a wire is used to drive the electrons from one side to the other and a Voltaic/Galvanic cell is created. It is an electrochemical cell that uses spontaneous redox reactions to generate electricity. A salt bridge also connects to the half-cells. The reading of the voltmeter gives the cell voltage or cell potential or electromotive force. If Ecell0 is positive the reaction is spontaneous and if it is negative the reaction is non-spontaneous and is referred to as electrolytic cell. Electrolysis refers to the decomposition of a substance by an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as Cu2+. This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis.
The conductance of material is the property of materials due to which a material allows the flow of ions through itself and thus conducts electricity. Conductivity is represented by k and it depends upon nature and concentration of electrolyte, temperature, etc. A more common term molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is conductance of the volume of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with the unit area of cross-section and distance of unit length. Limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes cannot be obtained graphically.

 5
(i)

Is silver plate the anode or cathode?

 1
(ii)

What will happen if the salt bridge is removed?

 1
(iii)

When does electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell?

 1
(iv)
  1. What will happen to the concentration of Zn2+ and Ag+ when Ecell = 0.
  2. Why does conductivity of a solution decreases with dilution?
 2

OR
(iv)

The molar conductivity of a 1.5 M solution of an electrolyte is found to be 138.9 Scm2mol-1. Calculate the conductivity of this solution.

 2

answer_key_html:
Annual Exam - 2022

Code: 56/5/2 (set-2)

  • Please check that this question paper contains 11 printed pages.
  • Q.P. Code given on the right hand side of the question paper should be written on the title page of the answer-book by the candidate.
  • Please check that this question paper contains 12 questions.
  • Please write down the Serial Number of the question in the answer-book before attempting it.
  • 15 minute time has been allotted to read this question paper. The question paper will be distributed at 10.15 a.m. From 10.15 a.m. to 10.30 a.m., the students will read the question paper only and will not write any answer on the answer-book during this period.

CHEMISTRY (Theory)

Time allowed:2 hours
Maximum Marks:35

General Instructions :

Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them:

  1. This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory.
  2. This question paper comprises of three sections- Section A, B and C.
  3. Section A - Q. No. 1 to 3 are very short-answer type questions carrying 2 marks each.
  4. Section B - Q. No. 4 to 11 are short-answer type questions carrying 3 marks each.
  5. Section C - Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks.
  6. Use of log tables and calculators is not allowed.

SECTION : A
1.

Arrange the following in the increasing order of their property indicated:

  1. Ethanal, Propanone, Propanal, Butanone (reactivity towards nucleophilic addition)
  2. 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, benzoic acid, 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid, 4-Methoxy benzoic acid (Acid strength)
Solution:
  1. The increasing order towards nucleophilic addition of the following compounds is Butanone < Propanone < Propanal < Ethanal (Due to +l effect).
  2. The increasing order of acidic strength of the following compounds is 4-methoxy benzoic acid < benzoic acid < 4-nitrobenzoic acid < 3,4-dinitrobenzoic acid. (Due to the presence of electron withdrawing group).
 2
2.

Explain the following reactions:

  1. Clemmensen reduction
  2. Stephen reaction
Solution:
  1. Clemmensen reduction
    Carbonyl compounds (aldehydes and ketones), that are not sensitive to acids, are heated with zinc amalgam and conc HCl to give alkanes. Carbonyl group is reduced to methylene group. This reaction is often used to convert acyl benzenes to alkyl benzene.
    {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{CH}_3+4[\mathrm{H}]{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Hg}/\;conc.\mathrm{HCl}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}{/tex}
  2. Stephen reaction
    Nitriles react with stannous chloride / HCl to form imine hydrochlorides. This is reduction reaction. The imine hydrochlirdes on acid hydrolysis gives aldehydes.
    {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}+2[\mathrm{H}]{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{SnCl}}_2/\mathrm{dilHC}1}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{NHHCl}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}{/tex}
 2
3.

Answer the following questions (Do any two):

  1. Identify the order of reaction from the following unit for its rate constant: Lmol-1s-1
  2. The conversion of molecules A to B follow second order kinetics. concentration of A is increased to three times, how will it affect the rate of formation of B?
  3. Write the expression of integrated rate equation for zero order reaction.
Solution:
  1. Second order
  2. Nine or 9 times
  3. k = {tex}\frac{[R]_0-[R]}{t}{/tex}
 2

SECTION : B
4.(a)
  1. Define coagulation.
  2. State Hardy-Schulze rule.
  3. What is Electrophoresis?
Solution:

Chapter Deleted from the Textbook.

 3

OR
(b)

Write three differences between Physisorption and Chemisorption.

Solution:

Chapter Delete from the Textbook.

 3
5.(a)
  1. Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: [Pt(NH3)6]Cl4
  2. On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration of d4 ion, if {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} < P.
  3. What are Heteroleptic complexes?
Solution:
  1. Hexaammineplatinum (IV) chloride
  2. {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}{ }^3 \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}{ }^1{/tex}
  3. Complexes in which a metal atom / ion is bound to more than one kind of donor groups / ligands.
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Using IUPAC norms write the formulas for the following:
    1. Pentaamminenitrito-N-Cobalt (III)
    2. Tetrahydroxidozincate (II)
  2. What is crystal field splitting energy?
Solution:
  1. Chemical formula of Pentaamminenitrito-N-cobalt(III) is [Co(NO2)(NH3)5]2+.
    Chemical formula of Tetrabromidocuprate(II) is [Cu(Br4)]2-.
  2. The crystal field stabilisation energy (CFSE) is the gain in the energy achieved by preferential filling up of orbitals by electrons. It is usually less than or equal to o. When it is equal to o, the complex is unstable. The magnitude of CFSE depends on the number and nature of ligands and the geometry of the complex.
 3
6.
  1. Write any two consequences of Lanthanoid Contraction.
  2. Name the element of 3d series which exhibits the largest number of oxidation states. Give reason.
Solution:
  1. The two consequences of lanthanide contraction are:
    1. Due to the close similarity in electronic configuration and ionic radii, the lanthanides have identical chemical properties which make their separation difficult.
    2. Due to lanthanide contraction, the size of Ln3+ ions decrease regularly with an increase in atomic number. According to Fajan's rule, a decrease in the size of Ln3+ ions increase the covalent character and decrease the basic character between Ln3+ and OH- ion in Ln(OH)3. Since the order of size of Ln3+ ions are La3+ > Ce3+ ... > Lu3+.
  2. The element which shows the largest number of oxidation states in the 3d series is Manganese (Mn). It shows the following oxidation states +2, +3, +4, +5, +6 and +7. It is because it contains the maximum no. of unpaired e-s in the outermost shell, i.e., 3d94s2.
 3
7.

Observe the graph shown in figure and answer the following questions:

  1. What is the order of the reaction?
  2. What is the slope of the curve?
  3. Write the relationship between k and t1/2 (half life period)
Solution:
  1. First order
  2. Slope = {tex}\frac{k}{2.303}{/tex}
  3. k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_\frac{1}{2}}{/tex}
 3
8.

Give reasons for the following statements:

  1. Scandium (Z = 21) is a transition element but Zn (Z = 30) is not.
  2. [Ti(H2O)]3+ is coloured while [Sc(H2 O)6]3+ is colourless.
  3. Physical and chemical properties of the 4d and 5d series of the transition elements are quite similar than expected.
Solution:
  1. On the basis of incompletely filled 3d-orbitals in case of Scandium atom (3d1) having one electron in its ground state, it is regarded as a transition element. On the other hand, zinc atom having completely filled d-orbitals (3d10) in its ground state as well as in its oxidized state, it is not considered as a transition element.
  2. Ti(H2O)]3+ is coloured because Ti3+(3d1) has one unpaired electron which undergoes a d-d transition so it is coloured. While Sc(H2O)6]3+ (3d0) has no unpaired electrons so it will not undergo d-d transition so it is colourless.
  3. Physical and chemical properties of 4d and 5d series elements are similar because they have same atomic and ionic radii due to the lanthanide contraction. Due to equal atomic radii the size of Zr and Hf, Nb and Ta, Mo and W, etc., the two elements of each pair have the same properties.
 3
9.(a)

Complete the following:

  1. {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{2\cdot{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{1. \mathrm{AlH}(\mathrm i-\mathrm{Bu})_2}{/tex} {tex}\text{ 'A' }\xrightarrow[\mathrm H^+]{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm N-\mathrm{OH}}\text{ 'B' }\\{/tex}
  2. Write IUPAC name of the following compound:
  3. Write chemical test to distinguish between the following compounds: Phenol and Benzoic acid
Solution:
  1.  
  2. 3-Bromobenzaldehyde / 3-Bromobenzene carbaldehyde
  3. Benzoic acid gives brisk effervescence liberating carbon dioxide on reaction with sodium hydrogen carbonate while phenol does not. / In terms of chemical reaction mentioning brisk effervescence.
 3

OR
(b)

Convert the following:

  1. Benzoic acid to Benzaldehyde
  2. Propan-1-ol to 2-Bromopropanoic acid
  3. Acetaldehyde to But-2-enal
Solution:
  2.  
  3. {tex}2\underset{Acetaldehyde}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CHO}}\xrightarrow{dil.\;\mathrm{NaOH}}\underset{\underset{Aldol\;condensation\;reaction}{Aldol\;(intermediate)}}{\left[{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{\overset{OH}\vert}{\mathrm{CH}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{CHO}-\right]}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{-{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]\Delta{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{But-2-enal}{\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}}-\mathrm{CHO}{/tex}
 3
10.

A primary amine A C2H7N reacts with alkyl halide (C2H5I) to give secondary amine B. B reacts with C6H5SO2Cl to give a solid C which is insoluble in alkali. Identify A, B, C and write all the chemical reactions involved.

Solution:

A = Ethyl amine,
B = Diethyl amine,
C = N, N-diethyl benzene sulphonamide
{tex}\mathrm{CH}\underset{1^{o\;}\underset{(A)}{Amine}}{{}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2N}H_2+{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm I\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm C\underset{\underset{(\mathrm B)}{\mathrm{Ethyl}\;\mathrm{iodide}}}{{\mathrm H}_2{\mathrm{NHCH}}_3}{\mathrm{CH}}_2+{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\mathrm{Cl}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\underset{\underset{(\mathrm C)}{\mathrm N,\;\mathrm N-\mathrm{diethyl}\;\mathrm{benzene}\;\mathrm{sulphonamide}}}{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\;\mathrm N\left({\mathrm C}_2{\mathrm H}_5\right)}{/tex}

 3
11.(a)

Account for the following:

  1. pkb of aniline is more than that of methylamine.
  2. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction.
  3. Primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines.
Solution:
  1. lone pair of electrons are delocalised over benzene ring due to resonance, electron density decreases and hence less available for protonation. 
  2. Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl3 to form a salt. 
  3. Presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding in primary amines which is absent in tertiary amines.
 3

OR
(b)
  1. Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their basic strength in aqueous solution:
    CH3 NH2, (CH3)3 N, (CH3)2 NH
  2. What is Hinsberg's reagent?
  3. What is the role of pyridine in the acylation reaction of amines?
Solution:
  1. The increasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution of the following amines is (CH3)3N < CH3NH2 < (CH3)2NH (Due to steric hindrance and +I effect of alky groups).
  2. Hinsberg reagent is also known as benzene sulphonyl chloride (C6H6SO2Cl). When it reacts with primary and secondary amines, it produces sulphonamides.
  3. Pyridine is used in the acylation of amines because it acts as a strong base which helps in removing the side product HCl from the reaction mixture. Therefore, it acts as an acceptor for the acid by-product formed during the reaction.
 3

SECTION : C
12.

Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow:

Oxidation-reduction reactions are commonly known as redox reactions. They involve transfer of electrons from one species to another. In a spontaneous reaction, energy is released which can be used to do useful work. The reaction is split into two half-reactions. Two different containers are used and a wire is used to drive the electrons from one side to the other and a Voltaic/Galvanic cell is created. It is an electrochemical cell that uses spontaneous redox reactions to generate electricity. A salt bridge also connects to the half-cells. The reading of the voltmeter gives the cell voltage or cell potential or electromotive force. If Ecell0 is positive the reaction is spontaneous and if it is negative the reaction is non-spontaneous and is referred to as electrolytic cell. Electrolysis refers to the decomposition of a substance by an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as Cu2+. This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis.
The conductance of material is the property of materials due to which a material allows the flow of ions through itself and thus conducts electricity. Conductivity is represented by k and it depends upon nature and concentration of electrolyte, temperature, etc. A more common term molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is conductance of the volume of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with the unit area of cross-section and distance of unit length. Limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes cannot be obtained graphically.

 5
(i)

Is silver plate the anode or cathode?

Solution:

Silver plate acts as cathode.

 1
(ii)

What will happen if the salt bridge is removed?

Solution:

Salt bridge permits the flow of current by completing the circuit as well as it maintains the charge balance between anode and cathode by movement of electrons. If the salt bridge is removed no current will flow in the circuit and the voltage will drop to zero.

 1
(iii)

When does electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell?

Solution:

An electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell when there is an application of an external opposite potential on the galvanic cell and reaction is not inhibited until the opposing voltage reaches the value 1.1 V. At this stage, no current flows through the cell and on increasing the external potential any further the reaction will function in the opposite direction.
Eext > Ecell

 1
(iv)
  1. What will happen to the concentration of Zn2+ and Ag+ when Ecell = 0.
  2. Why does conductivity of a solution decreases with dilution?
Solution:
  1. When Ecell = 0, an equilibrium condition is reached and the concentration of Zn2+ and Ag+ remains same.
  2. Conductivity of a solution defined as the conductance of ions present in a unit volume of the solution. On dilution, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Thus, the conductivity of the solution decreases on dilution.
 2

OR
(iv)

The molar conductivity of a 1.5 M solution of an electrolyte is found to be 138.9 Scm2mol-1. Calculate the conductivity of this solution.

Solution:

Molar Conductivity, k = {tex}\frac{\text { Conductivity }}{\text { Concentration }}{/tex}
{tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{c}}{/tex}
Conductivity {tex}=\Lambda_m \times \mathrm{C}{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{138.9\left(\mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \times 1.5(\mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L})}{1000\left(\mathrm{CM}^3 / \mathrm{L}\right)}{/tex}
= 0.208 Scm-1

 2

CHEMISTRY (Theory) Time allowed:2 hours Maximum Marks:35 General Instructions : Read the following instructions very carefully and strictly follow them: This question paper contains 12 questions. All questions are compulsory. This question paper comprises of three sections- Section A, B and C . Section A - Q. No. 1 to 3 are very short-answer type questions carrying 2 marks each. Section B - Q. No. 4 to 11 are short-answer type questions carrying 3 marks each. Section C - Q. No. 12 is case based question carrying 5 marks. Use of log tables and calculators is not allowed. SECTION : A 1. Arrange the following in the increasing order of their property indicated: Ethanal, Propanone, Propanal, Butanone (reactivity towards nucleophilic addition) 4-Nitrobenzoic acid, benzoic acid, 3,4-Dinitrobenzoic acid, 4-Methoxy benzoic acid (Acid strength) Solution: The increasing order towards nucleophilic addition of the following compounds is Butanone < Propanone < Propanal < Ethanal (Due to +l effect). The increasing order of acidic strength of the following compounds is 4-methoxy benzoic acid < benzoic acid < 4-nitrobenzoic acid < 3,4-dinitrobenzoic acid. (Due to the presence of electron withdrawing group). 2 2. Explain the following reactions: Clemmensen reduction Stephen reaction Solution: Clemmensen reduction Carbonyl compounds (aldehydes and ketones), that are not sensitive to acids, are heated with zinc amalgam and conc HCl to give alkanes. Carbonyl group is reduced to methylene group. This reaction is often used to convert acyl benzenes to alkyl benzene. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CO}-\mathrm{CH}_3+4[\mathrm{H}]{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{Zn}-\mathrm{Hg}/\;conc.\mathrm{HCl}}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CH}_2-\mathrm{CH}_3+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}{/tex} Stephen reaction Nitriles react with stannous chloride / HCl to form imine hydrochlorides. This is reduction reaction. The imine hydrochlirdes on acid hydrolysis gives aldehydes. {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{C} \equiv \mathrm{N}+2[\mathrm{H}]{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm{SnCl}}_2/\mathrm{dilHC}1}{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{NHHCl}\xrightarrow{{\mathrm H}_3\mathrm O^+}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{CH}_3-\mathrm{CHO}+\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}{/tex} 2 3. Write the expression of integrated rate equation for zero order reaction. Solution: Second order Nine or 9 times k = {tex}\frac{[R]_0-[R]}{t}{/tex} 2 SECTION : B 4. (a) Define coagulation. (a) Write the IUPAC name of the following complex: [Pt(NH 3 ) 6 ]Cl 4 On the basis of crystal field theory, write the electronic configuration of d 4 ion, if {tex}\Delta_o{/tex} < P. What are Heteroleptic complexes? Solution: Hexaammineplatinum (IV) chloride {tex}\mathrm{t}_{2 \mathrm{~g}}{ }^3 \mathrm{e}_{\mathrm{g}}{ }^1{/tex} Complexes in which a metal atom / ion is bound to more than one kind of donor groups / ligands. 3 OR (b) Using IUPAC norms write the formulas for the following: Pentaamminenitrito-N-Cobalt (III) Tetrahydroxidozincate (II) What is crystal field splitting energy? Solution: Chemical formula of Pentaamminenitrito-N-cobalt(III) is [Co(NO 2 )(NH 3 ) 5 ] 2+ . Chemical formula of Tetrabromidocuprate(II) is [Cu(Br 4 )] 2- . The crystal field stabilisation energy (CFSE) is the gain in the energy achieved by preferential filling up of orbitals by electrons. It is usually less than or equal to o. When it is equal to o, the complex is unstable. The magnitude of CFSE depends on the number and nature of ligands and the geometry of the complex. 3 6. Write any two consequences of Lanthanoid Contraction. Name the element of 3d series which exhibits the largest number of oxidation states. Give reason. Solution: The two consequences of lanthanide contraction are: Due to the close similarity in electronic configuration and ionic radii, the lanthanides have identical chemical properties which make their separation difficult. Due to lanthanide contraction, the size of Ln 3+ ions decrease regularly with an increase in atomic number. According to Fajan's rule, a decrease in the size of Ln 3+ ions increase the covalent character and decrease the basic character between Ln 3+ and OH - ion in Ln(OH) 3 . Since the order of size of Ln 3+ ions are La 3+ > Ce 3+ ... > Lu 3+ . The element which shows the largest number of oxidation states in the 3d series is Manganese (Mn). It shows the following oxidation states +2, +3, +4, +5, +6 and +7. It is because it contains the maximum no. of unpaired e - s in the outermost shell, i.e., 3d 9 4s 2 . 3 7. Observe the graph shown in figure and answer the following questions: What is the order of the reaction? What is the slope of the curve? Write the relationship between k and t 1/2 (half life period) Solution: First order Slope = {tex}\frac{k}{2.303}{/tex} k = {tex}\frac{0.693}{t_\frac{1}{2}}{/tex} 3 8. Give reasons for the following statements: Scandium (Z = 21) is a transition element but Zn (Z = 30) is not. [Ti(H 2 O)] 3+ is coloured while [Sc(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ is colourless. Physical and chemical properties of the 4d and 5d series of the transition elements are quite similar than expected. Solution: On the basis of incompletely filled 3d-orbitals in case of Scandium atom (3d 1 ) having one electron in its ground state, it is regarded as a transition element. On the other hand, zinc atom having completely filled d-orbitals (3d 10 ) in its ground state as well as in its oxidized state, it is not considered as a transition element. Ti(H 2 O)] 3+ is coloured because Ti 3+ (3d 1 ) has one unpaired electron which undergoes a d-d transition so it is coloured. While Sc(H 2 O) 6 ] 3+ (3d 0 ) has no unpaired electrons so it will not undergo d-d transition so it is colourless. Physical and chemical properties of 4d and 5d series elements are similar because they have same atomic and ionic radii due to the lanthanide contraction. Due to equal atomic radii the size of Zr and Hf, Nb and Ta, Mo and W, etc., the two elements of each pair have the same properties. 3 9. (a) Complete the following: {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm{CN}\xrightarrow[{2\cdot{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]{1. \mathrm{AlH}(\mathrm i-\mathrm{Bu})_2}{/tex} {tex}\text{ 'A' }\xrightarrow[\mathrm H^+]{{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm N-\mathrm{OH}}\text{ 'B' }\\{/tex} Write IUPAC name of the following compound: Write chemical test to distinguish between the following compounds: Phenol and Benzoic acid Solution: 3-Bromobenzaldehyde / 3-Bromobenzene carbaldehyde Benzoic acid gives brisk effervescence liberating carbon dioxide on reaction with sodium hydrogen carbonate while phenol does not. / In terms of chemical reaction mentioning brisk effervescence. 3 OR (b) Convert the following: Benzoic acid to Benzaldehyde Propan-1-ol to 2-Bromopropanoic acid Acetaldehyde to But-2-enal Solution: {tex}2\underset{Acetaldehyde}{{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\mathrm{CHO}}\xrightarrow{dil.\;\mathrm{NaOH}}\underset{\underset{Aldol\;condensation\;reaction}{Aldol\;(intermediate)}}{\left[{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\overset{\overset{OH}\vert}{\mathrm{CH}}-{\mathrm{CH}}_2-\mathrm{CHO}-\right]}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow[{-{\mathrm H}_2\mathrm O}]\Delta{\mathrm{CH}}_3-\underset{But-2-enal}{\mathrm{CH}=\mathrm{CH}}-\mathrm{CHO}{/tex} 3 10. A primary amine A C 2 H 7 N reacts with alkyl halide (C 2 H 5 I) to give secondary amine B . B reacts with C 6 H 5 SO 2 Cl to give a solid C which is insoluble in alkali. Identify A , B , C and write all the chemical reactions involved. Solution: A = Ethyl amine, B = Diethyl amine, C = N, N-diethyl benzene sulphonamide {tex}\mathrm{CH}\underset{1^{o\;}\underset{(A)}{Amine}}{{}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2N}H_2+{\mathrm{CH}}_3{\mathrm{CH}}_2\mathrm I\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}{\mathrm{CH}}_3\mathrm C\underset{\underset{(\mathrm B)}{\mathrm{Ethyl}\;\mathrm{iodide}}}{{\mathrm H}_2{\mathrm{NHCH}}_3}{\mathrm{CH}}_2+{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\mathrm{Cl}\longrightarrow{/tex} {tex}\underset{\underset{(\mathrm C)}{\mathrm N,\;\mathrm N-\mathrm{diethyl}\;\mathrm{benzene}\;\mathrm{sulphonamide}}}{{\mathrm C}_6{\mathrm H}_5{\mathrm{SO}}_2\;\mathrm N\left({\mathrm C}_2{\mathrm H}_5\right)}{/tex} 3 11. (a) Account for the following: pk b of aniline is more than that of methylamine. Aniline does not undergo Friedel-Crafts reaction. Primary amines have higher boiling points than tertiary amines. Solution: lone pair of electrons are delocalised over benzene ring due to resonance, electron density decreases and hence less available for protonation. Aniline is a Lewis base and it reacts with AlCl 3 to form a salt. Presence of intermolecular hydrogen bonding in primary amines which is absent in tertiary amines. 3 OR (b) Arrange the following compounds in the increasing order of their basic strength in aqueous solution: CH 3 NH 2 , (CH 3 ) 3 N, (CH 3 ) 2 NH What is Hinsberg's reagent? What is the role of pyridine in the acylation reaction of amines? Solution: The increasing order of basic strength in aqueous solution of the following amines is (CH 3 ) 3 N < CH 3 NH 2 < (CH 3 ) 2 NH (Due to steric hindrance and +I effect of alky groups). Hinsberg reagent is also known as benzene sulphonyl chloride (C 6 H 6 SO 2 Cl). When it reacts with primary and secondary amines, it produces sulphonamides. Pyridine is used in the acylation of amines because it acts as a strong base which helps in removing the side product HCl from the reaction mixture. Therefore, it acts as an acceptor for the acid by-product formed during the reaction. 3 SECTION : C 12. Read the passage given below and answer the questions that follow: Oxidation-reduction reactions are commonly known as redox reactions. They involve transfer of electrons from one species to another. In a spontaneous reaction, energy is released which can be used to do useful work. The reaction is split into two half-reactions. Two different containers are used and a wire is used to drive the electrons from one side to the other and a Voltaic/Galvanic cell is created. It is an electrochemical cell that uses spontaneous redox reactions to generate electricity. A salt bridge also connects to the half-cells. The reading of the voltmeter gives the cell voltage or cell potential or electromotive force. If E cell 0 is positive the reaction is spontaneous and if it is negative the reaction is non-spontaneous and is referred to as electrolytic cell. Electrolysis refers to the decomposition of a substance by an electric current. One mole of electric charge when passed through a cell will discharge half a mole of a divalent metal ion such as Cu 2+ . This was first formulated by Faraday in the form of laws of electrolysis. The conductance of material is the property of materials due to which a material allows the flow of ions through itself and thus conducts electricity. Conductivity is represented by k and it depends upon nature and concentration of electrolyte, temperature, etc. A more common term molar conductivity of a solution at a given concentration is conductance of the volume of solution containing one mole of electrolyte kept between two electrodes with the unit area of cross-section and distance of unit length. Limiting molar conductivity of weak electrolytes cannot be obtained graphically. 5 (i) Is silver plate the anode or cathode? Solution: Silver plate acts as cathode. 1 (ii) What will happen if the salt bridge is removed? Solution: Salt bridge permits the flow of current by completing the circuit as well as it maintains the charge balance between anode and cathode by movement of electrons. If the salt bridge is removed no current will flow in the circuit and the voltage will drop to zero. 1 (iii) When does electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell? Solution: An electrochemical cell behaves like an electrolytic cell when there is an application of an external opposite potential on the galvanic cell and reaction is not inhibited until the opposing voltage reaches the value 1.1 V. At this stage, no current flows through the cell and on increasing the external potential any further the reaction will function in the opposite direction. E ext > E cell 1 (iv) What will happen to the concentration of Zn 2+ and Ag + when E cell = 0. Why does conductivity of a solution decreases with dilution? Solution: When E cell = 0, an equilibrium condition is reached and the concentration of Zn 2+ and Ag + remains same. Conductivity of a solution defined as the conductance of ions present in a unit volume of the solution. On dilution, the number of ions per unit volume decreases. Thus, the conductivity of the solution decreases on dilution. 2 OR (iv) The molar conductivity of a 1.5 M solution of an electrolyte is found to be 138.9 Scm 2 mol -1 . Calculate the conductivity of this solution. Solution: Molar Conductivity, k = {tex}\frac{\text { Conductivity }}{\text { Concentration }}{/tex} {tex}\Lambda_m=\frac{\mathrm{K}}{\mathrm{c}}{/tex} Conductivity {tex}=\Lambda_m \times \mathrm{C}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{138.9\left(\mathrm{Scm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right) \times 1.5(\mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L})}{1000\left(\mathrm{CM}^3 / \mathrm{L}\right)}{/tex} = 0.208 Scm -1 2
